risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following consent, participants were randomly assigned to condition using a computerized blocked randomization method." Comment: This explicitly describes a random allocation procedure using a computerized blocked method, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "Following consent, participants were randomly assigned to condition using a computerized blocked randomization method." Comment: The paper describes the randomization sequence but gives no explicit information about how allocation was concealed from the recruiter/enroller (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical containers), so foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The report describes randomization to RRFT versus treatment as usual and details therapist supervision/adherence, but it does not state whether participants, therapists, or other personnel were blinded to allocation. In a psychotherapy trial, blinding of participants and treating personnel is generally not feasible, but the paper does not explicitly confirm this, and the outcomes include self-report and family-report measures that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of treatment assignment. Comment: Because the study does not specify who was blinded or whether blinding was attempted/broken, the most defensible judgement for this domain is unclear risk.

Summary: The outcomes were assessed mainly by patient self-report and caregiver/parent report instruments, plus interview-based substance use reporting. The paper states: “Assessment measures were re-administered at post-treatment, 3-, and 6-month follow-up,” and the dependent measures include “the UCLA PTSD Index for DSM-IV-Adolescent & Caregiver versions,” “The Child Depression Inventory (CDI) … was used to assess depression symptoms,” “The Behavioral Assessment System for Children (BASC-2) (parent and youth self-report),” “The Time Line Follow Back Interview (TLFB), a well-established method of assessing SU … was conducted with each participant,” and “The Cohesion and Conflict subscales of the Family Environment Scale (FES) … were completed by adolescents and caregivers.” Because these are subjective outcomes and the participant/caregiver is effectively the assessor, lack of blinding could clearly influence measurement; no blinded outcome assessor is described. The study also reports clinician/judgement-free objective validation (urine drug screens), but the outcome set includes multiple subjective outcomes, so the domain is judged on the most vulnerable outcomes.

Quote: "Data were comprised of four repeated measurements (level-1) nested within 30 families (level-2)" and Table 2 reports models with df based on all 30 randomized families; the paper also states "Assessment measures were re-administered at post-treatment, 3-, and 6-month follow-up" and provides outcome data at each time point in Table 1. Comment: The article presents repeated-measures mixed-effects models using all randomized participants/families and does not report substantial missing outcome data or differential loss to follow-up. With no evidence of ≥5% overall loss, no arm imbalance, and no warning sign of exclusion or per-protocol analysis, attrition bias is unlikely.
